questions.question: G is a graph on n vertices and 2n - 2 edges. The edges of G can be partitioned into two edge-disjoint spanning trees. Which of the following is NOT true for G? A. For every subset of k vertices, the induced subgraph has at most 2k - 2 edges. B. The minimum cut in G has at least 2 edges. C. There are at least 2 edge-disjoint paths between every pair of vertices. D. There are at least 2 vertex-disjoint paths between every pair of vertices.
questions.answer: D. There are at least 2 vertex-disjoint paths between every pair of vertices.
questions.to_find: Which statement is NOT guaranteed for a graph that splits into two edge-disjoint spanning trees.

understand.plain: Each spanning tree on n vertices uses exactly n - 1 edges, so two of them use 2n - 2 edges - exactly what G has. Having two edge-disjoint spanning trees forces G to be 2-edge-connected (you can never cut it with a single edge), but it does NOT force 2-vertex-connectivity. So options A, B, C all hold, and the vertex-disjoint claim (D) can fail.

keywords.explain: a connected, cycle-free subgraph that touches all n vertices using n - 1 edges
keywords.example: on 4 vertices any spanning tree has 3 edges

keywords.explain: the two trees share no edge (vertices may be shared)
keywords.example: tree1 and tree2 together give 2(n-1) = 2n - 2 distinct edges

keywords.explain: you must delete at least k edges (or k vertices) to disconnect the graph; edge-connectivity is the weaker condition
keywords.example: two triangles glued at one vertex are 2-edge-connected but only 1-vertex-connected

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Two triangles sharing one cut vertex'><title>2-edge-connected but not 2-vertex-connected</title><desc>Two triangles glued at a single vertex form a graph with two edge-disjoint spanning trees, yet removing that shared vertex disconnects it, so paths need not be vertex-disjoint.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='70' x2='90' y2='190' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='70' x2='280' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='190' x2='280' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='470' y1='70' x2='470' y2='190' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='470' y1='70' x2='280' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='470' y1='190' x2='280' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='90' cy='70' r='16' class='bg'/><circle cx='90' cy='190' r='16' class='bg'/><circle cx='470' cy='70' r='16' class='bg'/><circle cx='470' cy='190' r='16' class='bg'/><circle cx='280' cy='130' r='18' class='no'/><text x='280' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>cut vertex</text><text x='280' y='235' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>2-edge-connected, but deleting the middle vertex splits it</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two triangles sharing one vertex: 2-edge-connected (option C holds) yet removing the shared vertex disconnects it, so option D fails.

given.aim: Decide which property a graph with two edge-disjoint spanning trees need not satisfy.

terms.connects: each spanning tree has n - 1 edges
exactly two trees' worth, so no extra edges remain

terms.meaning: minimum edges to delete to disconnect G
terms.connects: guarantees options B and C by Menger's theorem

terms.meaning: minimum vertices to delete to disconnect G
terms.connects: this is what option D wrongly assumes is at least 2

given.plan: Use that two edge-disjoint spanning trees make G 2-edge-connected (so A, B, C hold), then exhibit a cut vertex showing the vertex-disjoint claim D can fail.

steps.title: Edge count fits two spanning trees
steps.formula_raw: edges in a tree on n vertices = n - 1
steps.apply: 2 trees -> 2(n - 1) = 2n - 2 = |E(G)|, so the two trees use up every edge
steps.note: G is the exact union of two edge-disjoint spanning trees

steps.title: Why A, B, C are true
steps.apply: Any k vertices carry at most (k-1) edges in each tree -> at most 2k - 2 total (A). Two edge-disjoint trees keep G connected after any single edge is cut, so min cut >= 2 (B) and there are >= 2 edge-disjoint paths between any pair by Menger (C).

steps.title: Why D fails
steps.apply: Two triangles sharing one vertex still split into two edge-disjoint spanning trees, yet that shared vertex is a cut vertex - so two vertices on opposite sides have NO two vertex-disjoint paths.

solution.result: Option D is NOT true: vertex-disjoint paths are not guaranteed.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: two edge-disjoint spanning trees force 2-edge-connectivity (A, B, C) but not 2-vertex-connectivity, so D is the false statement.

questions.question: The line graph L(G) of a simple graph G is defined as follows: There is exactly one vertex v(e) in L(G) for each edge e in G. For any two edges e and e' in G, L(G) has an edge between v(e) and v(e'), if and only if e and e' are incident with the same vertex in G. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? (P) The line graph of a cycle is a cycle. (Q) The line graph of a clique is a clique. (R) The line graph of a planar graph is planar. (S) The line graph of a tree is a tree. A. P only. B. P and R only. C. R only. D. P, Q and S only.
questions.to_find: Which of the four line-graph statements P, Q, R, S are true.

understand.plain: In a line graph, each edge of G becomes a vertex, and two such vertices are joined when the original edges shared an endpoint. Check each claim with the smallest example: a cycle's line graph is again a cycle (P true), but a clique, a planar graph and a tree all break, because edges meeting at a high-degree vertex form a big clique in L(G).

keywords.explain: vertices = edges of G; two are adjacent when the edges touch a common vertex
keywords.example: edges of a path a-b-c share vertex b, so their L(G)-vertices are joined

keywords.explain: every pair of its n vertices is joined; it has n(n-1)/2 edges
one center joined to m leaves - a special tree
keywords.example: K_{1,3} has 3 edges all meeting at the center

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 270' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Line graph of a star is a triangle'><title>Line graph of the star K_{1,3}</title><desc>A star with three edges meeting at one center turns into a triangle in the line graph, so the line graph of a tree need not be a tree.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='150' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>tree K_(1,3)</text><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='130' x2='70' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='130' x2='150' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='130' x2='230' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='150' cy='130' r='15' class='bg'/><circle cx='70' cy='200' r='13' class='bg'/><circle cx='150' cy='210' r='13' class='bg'/><circle cx='230' cy='200' r='13' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='155' class='sub mut'>e1</text><text x='160' y='180' class='sub mut'>e2</text><text x='190' y='155' class='sub mut'>e3</text><text x='300' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='big mut'>-></text><text x='460' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>line graph = K_3</text><line class='edge' x1='460' y1='110' x2='400' y2='210' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='460' y1='110' x2='520' y2='210' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='400' y1='210' x2='520' y2='210' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='460' cy='110' r='14' class='hi'/><circle cx='400' cy='210' r='14' class='hi'/><circle cx='520' cy='210' r='14' class='hi'/><text x='460' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>e2</text><text x='385' y='235' class='sub ink'>e1</text><text x='525' y='235' class='sub ink'>e3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The star K_{1,3} (a tree) has a line graph equal to the triangle K_3, showing statement S is false; similar clique blow-ups break Q and R.

given.aim: Test each statement P, Q, R, S about line graphs and keep only the true ones.

terms.connects: each edge meets two neighbours -> still a cycle (TRUE)

terms.example: L(K_4) has 6 vertices, not a clique
terms.connects: edges of K_n are not all mutually incident (FALSE)

terms.meaning: line graph of a planar graph
terms.example: K_{1,5} is planar but L(K_{1,5}) = K_5 is non-planar
terms.connects: a high-degree vertex creates a large clique (FALSE)

terms.example: L(K_{1,3}) = triangle K_3, which has a cycle
terms.connects: a star is a tree but its line graph contains a cycle (FALSE)

given.plan: Verify P directly on a cycle, then find one small counterexample each for Q, R, S using a star or clique to refute them.

steps.title: P: line graph of a cycle
steps.formula_raw: v(e) ~ v(e') in L(G) iff e, e' share an endpoint
steps.apply: In C_n each edge shares an endpoint with exactly 2 neighbours, so L(C_n) is a cycle C_n -> P is TRUE

steps.title: Refute Q and R with a star
steps.formula_raw: edges meeting at a degree-d vertex form a clique K_d in L(G)
steps.apply: L(K_4) is not a clique (Q false). K_{1,5} is planar yet L(K_{1,5}) = K_5 is non-planar (R false)
steps.note: high degree in G breeds large cliques in L(G)

steps.title: Refute S with K_{1,3}
steps.apply: The tree K_{1,3} gives L = K_3, a triangle (contains a cycle), so it is not a tree -> S is FALSE

solution.result: Only P is true -> Option A.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: edges incident to a common vertex become a clique in L(G); this confirms P and breaks Q, R, S, so the answer is P only.

questions.question: Consider an undirected graph G where self-loops are not allowed. The vertex set of G is {(i, j) | 1 <= i <= 12, 1 <= j <= 12}. There is an edge between (a, b) and (c, d) if |a - c| <= 1 and |b - d| <= 1. The number of edges in this graph is ______.
questions.to_find: Total number of edges in this 12 x 12 grid (king-move) graph.

understand.plain: Picture a 12 x 12 grid of cells. Two cells are joined exactly when they are at most one step apart in both the row and the column - that is, like a king's move in chess (horizontal, vertical, or diagonal). Count the king moves: horizontal, vertical, and the two diagonal directions, then add them up.

keywords.explain: a cell sitting in row i and column j of a 12 x 12 board
two cells are joined when both row gap and column gap are at most 1 (so 8 neighbours max)
count each edge type (horizontal, vertical, two diagonals) as adjacent pairs to avoid double counting

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='King-move adjacencies on a grid'><title>King moves on a 12x12 grid</title><desc>A center cell connects to its 8 king-neighbours: horizontal and vertical neighbours plus the four diagonals; totalling edges by direction gives 506.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><g><rect x='200' y='40' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='270' y='40' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='340' y='40' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='200' y='110' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='270' y='110' width='60' height='60' class='hi'/><rect x='340' y='110' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='200' y='180' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='270' y='180' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='340' y='180' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/></g><line x1='300' y1='140' x2='230' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='300' y1='140' x2='300' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='300' y1='140' x2='370' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='300' y1='140' x2='230' y2='140' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='300' y1='140' x2='370' y2='140' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='300' y1='140' x2='230' y2='210' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='300' y1='140' x2='300' y2='210' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='300' y1='140' x2='370' y2='210' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='300' y='278' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>H 132 + V 132 + 2 diagonals 242 = 506</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A center cell links to its 8 king-neighbours; summing horizontal (132), vertical (132) and the two diagonal families (242) gives 506 edges.

given.aim: Count all unordered adjacent pairs in the 12 x 12 king-move grid.

terms.connects: edges counted per row/column of length 12

given.plan: Add edges by direction: horizontal + vertical + both diagonals, each counted once as adjacent cell pairs.

steps.title: Horizontal and vertical edges
steps.note: 11 adjacent pairs along each line of 12 cells

steps.title: Diagonal edges (both directions)
steps.note: each interior 2x2 block contributes one '/' and one '\' diagonal

questions.formula_note: Key idea: an m x n king-graph has m(n-1) + n(m-1) + 2(m-1)(n-1) edges; for 12 x 12 this is 132 + 132 + 242 = 506.

questions.question: The maximum number of edges in a bipartite graph on 12 vertices is ______.
questions.to_find: Largest possible edge count of a bipartite graph with 12 vertices.

understand.plain: Split the 12 vertices into two groups and join every vertex in one group to every vertex in the other (a complete bipartite graph). The product of the two group sizes is largest when the split is as even as possible: 6 and 6, giving 6 x 6 = 36 edges.

keywords.explain: vertices split into two sets with edges only between the sets, never inside one
every vertex on one side is joined to every vertex on the other; it has m x n edges
for a fixed total, the product m x n peaks when m and n are as equal as possible

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Complete bipartite split 6 and 6'><title>K_{6,6} maximises edges</title><desc>Splitting 12 vertices as 6 and 6 and joining every left vertex to every right vertex gives 6 x 6 = 36 edges, the maximum for a bipartite graph on 12 vertices.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><g stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5' opacity='0.6'><line x1='150' y1='50' x2='410' y2='50'/><line x1='150' y1='50' x2='410' y2='90'/><line x1='150' y1='50' x2='410' y2='130'/><line x1='150' y1='90' x2='410' y2='50'/><line x1='150' y1='90' x2='410' y2='170'/><line x1='150' y1='130' x2='410' y2='90'/><line x1='150' y1='130' x2='410' y2='210'/><line x1='150' y1='170' x2='410' y2='130'/><line x1='150' y1='210' x2='410' y2='170'/><line x1='150' y1='250' x2='410' y2='210'/><line x1='150' y1='250' x2='410' y2='250'/></g><text x='150' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Set A (6)</text><text x='410' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Set B (6)</text><circle cx='150' cy='50' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='150' cy='90' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='150' cy='130' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='150' cy='170' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='150' cy='210' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='150' cy='250' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='410' cy='50' r='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='410' cy='90' r='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='410' cy='130' r='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='410' cy='170' r='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='410' cy='210' r='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='410' cy='250' r='10' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='290' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>6 x 6 = 36 edges (max)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Twelve vertices split evenly 6 and 6 in a complete bipartite K_{6,6}, giving the maximum 6 x 6 = 36 edges.

given.aim: Maximise edges of a bipartite graph whose two parts together have 12 vertices.

terms.connects: fixed sum to be split into two parts
one factor of the edge product
other factor; edges = m x n

given.plan: Use K_{m,n} edges = m x n with m + n = 12, and maximise the product by balancing the split.

steps.title: Edges of a complete bipartite graph
steps.formula_raw: edges(K_{m,n}) = m x n with m + n = total
steps.apply: we want to maximise m x n subject to m + n = 12
steps.note: every cross-pair is an edge; same-side pairs are forbidden

steps.title: Balance the split
steps.formula_raw: max m x n at m = n = total/2 -> floor(total^2 / 4)

questions.formula_note: Key idea: a bipartite graph on v vertices has at most floor(v^2 / 4) edges; for v = 12 this is 36, attained by the balanced K_{6,6}.

questions.question: If G is the forest with n vertices and k connected components, how many edges does G have? A. floor(n/k). B. ceil(n/k). C. n - k. D. n - k + 1.
questions.to_find: Number of edges in a forest of n vertices with k components.

understand.plain: A forest is a collection of trees, and each tree on its own vertices has one fewer edge than vertices. Adding the (vertices - 1) over all k trees, the '-1's pile up to '-k', so the total is n - k edges.

keywords.explain: a graph with no cycles; each connected piece is a tree
keywords.example: three separate trees form a forest with 3 components

keywords.explain: a maximal connected chunk - here, one whole tree
a tree on p vertices has exactly p - 1 edges
keywords.example: a tree of 5 vertices has 4 edges

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Forest with three trees'><title>Forest: edges = n - k</title><desc>A forest of three trees with seven vertices in total has seven minus three equals four edges, illustrating edges = n - k.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='80' y1='70' x2='50' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='80' y1='70' x2='120' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='80' cy='70' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='50' cy='150' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='120' cy='150' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='85' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>3 vtx, 2 edges</text><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='70' x2='280' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='280' cy='70' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='280' cy='150' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='280' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2 vtx, 1 edge</text><line class='edge' x1='470' y1='70' x2='470' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='470' cy='70' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='470' cy='150' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='470' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2 vtx, 1 edge</text><text x='280' y='235' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>n = 7, k = 3  ->  edges = 7 - 3 = 4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three trees totalling seven vertices have four edges, matching edges = n - k = 7 - 3.

given.aim: Express the edge count of a forest in terms of n and k.

terms.connects: sum of the trees' vertex counts
each tree contributes one '-1'

terms.meaning: p_i - 1 for a tree of p_i vertices
terms.connects: summed over all k trees

given.plan: Sum the per-tree edge counts (p_i - 1) over all k trees and use that the p_i add up to n.

steps.title: Edges of each tree
steps.apply: sum over i: sum (p_i - 1) = (sum p_i) - k
steps.note: p_i = vertices of the i-th tree

steps.title: Substitute the vertex total
steps.apply: sum p_i = n, so total edges = n - k
steps.note: the k subtracted ones come from the k trees

questions.formula_note: Key idea: a forest with n vertices and k components has exactly n - k edges (each tree contributes vertices minus one).

questions.question: In a connected graph, a bridge is an edge whose removal disconnects the graph. Which one of the following statements is true? A. A tree has no bridges. B. A bridge cannot be part of a simple cycle. C. Every edge of a clique with size >= 3 is a bridge (A clique is any complete subgraph of a graph). D. A graph with bridges cannot have a cycle.
questions.answer: B. A bridge cannot be part of a simple cycle.
questions.to_find: The single true statement about bridges in a connected graph.

understand.plain: A bridge is an edge with no backup route - cutting it splits the graph. If an edge lay on a cycle, the rest of the cycle would still connect its endpoints, so it would not be a bridge. That makes B true. The other options fail: tree edges are all bridges (so A is false), clique edges with size >= 3 lie on triangles so they are NOT bridges (C false), and a graph can have both bridges and cycles in different parts (D false).

keywords.explain: an edge whose removal increases the number of components
keywords.example: the single link joining two clusters

keywords.explain: a closed walk with no repeated vertices; every edge on it has an alternate path
a complete subgraph; with 3+ vertices every edge sits on a triangle
keywords.example: in K_3 each edge is part of the triangle

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Bridge versus cycle edge'><title>A bridge cannot lie on a cycle</title><desc>The middle edge joining two triangles is a bridge (removing it disconnects the graph), while edges inside each triangle lie on a cycle and are not understand.visual_alt: Two triangles joined by a single edge: that joining edge is a bridge and lies on no cycle, while triangle edges sit on a cycle and are not bridges.

given.aim: Identify the one correct statement among A-D about bridges.

terms.meaning: claims a tree has no bridges
terms.example: but every tree edge is a bridge
terms.connects: FALSE - removing any tree edge disconnects it

terms.meaning: a bridge is on no simple cycle
terms.connects: TRUE - this is the defining characterisation

terms.meaning: every edge of K_m, m >= 3, is a bridge
terms.example: edges of K_3 lie on a triangle
terms.connects: FALSE - clique edges are on cycles, not bridges

terms.meaning: a graph with bridges has no cycle
terms.example: two triangles joined by a bridge
terms.connects: FALSE - bridges and cycles can coexist

given.plan: Apply the bridge-cycle theorem (an edge is a bridge iff it lies on no cycle) to confirm B and refute A, C, D with quick examples.

steps.title: Characterise a bridge
steps.formula_raw: edge e is a bridge  <=>  e lies on no cycle of G
steps.apply: If e were on a simple cycle, the remaining cycle path would still connect its endpoints, so e could not be a bridge -> B is TRUE
steps.note: this is the standard bridge characterisation

steps.title: Refute A and C
steps.formula_raw: tree edge -> on no cycle -> bridge;  clique edge (m>=3) -> on a triangle -> not a bridge
steps.apply: Every edge of a tree is a bridge (A false). In K_m with m >= 3 each edge lies on a triangle, so none is a bridge (C false)
steps.note: directly opposite to what A and C claim

steps.title: Refute D
steps.apply: Two triangles joined by one edge: that edge is a bridge, yet the graph still contains two cycles, so D is false
steps.note: bridges and cycles can live in the same graph

solution.result: Only B is true: a bridge cannot be part of a simple cycle -> Option B.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: an edge is a bridge if and only if it lies on no cycle, which directly makes B true and refutes A, C, and D.

formulas.name: Edges in a Tree
formulas.plain: a tree on n vertices has exactly n - 1 edges
formulas.whenToUse: whenever a connected acyclic graph (tree) or a spanning tree's edge count is needed
formulas.trap: this holds only for a single tree; for a forest of k components use n - k instead
formulas.reference: Tree fundamentals (a connected graph with n vertices and n - 1 edges is a tree)

formulas.name: Edges in a Forest
formulas.plain: a forest with n vertices and k components (trees) has n - k edges
formulas.whenToUse: to count edges of an acyclic graph that may be disconnected (several trees)
formulas.trap: setting k = 1 recovers a single tree's n - 1; do not confuse k with the number of edges
formulas.reference: Forest = disjoint union of k trees, each contributing (vertices - 1) edges

formulas.name: Maximum Edges in a Bipartite Graph
formulas.plain: a bipartite graph on v vertices has at most floor(v^2 / 4) edges, achieved by the balanced complete bipartite graph K_{m,n} with m + n = v
formulas.whenToUse: to bound or maximise edges when a graph must be bipartite (two-colourable, no odd cycle)

terms.means: total number of vertices, v = m + n
sizes of the two parts; edges = m x n

formulas.trap: the product m x n is largest at the balanced split (m and n as equal as possible), not at any lopsided split
formulas.reference: Complete bipartite graph K_{m,n} has m x n edges; maximise under m + n = v

formulas.name: Edges in an m x n King-Move Grid Graph
formulas.plain: for an m x n grid where cells are adjacent when both coordinate differences are at most 1 (king moves), the edges are horizontal m(n-1) + vertical n(m-1) + two diagonal families 2(m-1)(n-1)
formulas.whenToUse: counting edges of grid graphs with 8-neighbour (king) adjacency
formulas.trap: do not forget the two diagonal directions; counting only rook (horizontal+vertical) moves undercounts the edges
formulas.reference: King graph on an m x n board; adjacency |a - c| <= 1 and |b - d| <= 1

formulas.name: Line Graph Adjacency Rule
formulas.plain: in the line graph L(G), each edge of G becomes a vertex, and two such vertices are adjacent exactly when the original edges share an endpoint
formulas.whenToUse: when reasoning about line graphs L(G), e.g. whether structure is preserved

terms.means: the vertex of L(G) representing edge e

formulas.trap: the d edges meeting at a degree-d vertex form a clique K_d in L(G), so high degree creates large cliques (L of a tree/planar graph need not be a tree/planar)

formulas.name: Bridge-Cycle Criterion
formulas.latex: e \text{ is a bridge} \iff e \text{ lies on no cycle}
formulas.plain: an edge is a bridge (cut edge) if and only if it does not lie on any cycle of the graph
formulas.whenToUse: to test whether an edge is a bridge, or to reason about cut edges versus cycles

terms.means: an edge whose removal increases the number of components

formulas.trap: every tree edge is a bridge; conversely an edge on a triangle or any cycle is never a bridge (so clique edges with size >= 3 are not bridges)
formulas.reference: Standard characterisation of cut edges in graph theory
